planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown shrubs in moist to dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral or alkaline soil in full sun; eastern groundsel tree is extremely adaptable — tolerating salt spray, flooding, drought, clay, sand, and alkaline conditions. It can become weedy by seeding; female plants produce the seed, so male plants are less weedy.

directions: Plant in almost any well-drained to moist soil in full sun to part shade; eastern groundsel tree is a large, fast-growing, fall-blooming native shrub of coastal and inland wetland edges from Massachusetts to Texas, valued primarily for its extraordinary fall display when female plants produce dense, silky-white seed heads (pappi) in such abundance that the plant appears to be covered in brilliant white cotton or snow. Male plants produce the inconspicuous but nectar-rich cream-white flowers in September and October that attract monarch butterflies, native bees, and insects before seed set. It is among the most salt-tolerant and flood-tolerant native shrubs of the Atlantic and Gulf Coasts — growing in coastal marshes, salt spray environments, and periodically inundated soils where few ornamentals survive. Equally valuable inland as a fast-growing screen or wildlife shelter shrub. The brilliant white fall seed heads of female plants are stunning in the autumn landscape. A critical ecological shrub for the coastal plain.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall where plants are to grow, or allow existing plants to self-sow freely; white fumewort self-sows into cracks and crevices in walls, steps, and paving where it often thrives better than in cultivated soil. It tolerates dry shade under trees. Unlike blue-flowered C. flexuosa, this species blooms continuously from spring through fall without a summer rest.

directions: Sow seeds in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part to full shade; white fumewort is one of the finest and most long-blooming shade perennials available — a European native producing delicate, finely divided, blue-green foliage resembling bleeding heart or maidenhair fern, topped continuously from April through November with clusters of small, tubular flowers in creamy-white with a yellow tip. Unlike many shade perennials with limited bloom seasons, this species is genuinely in flower for most of the growing season in mild climates, filling the shade garden with a constant veil of soft white. It is a natural wall and pavement plant, spontaneously colonizing cracks in stone, brick, and mortar where it often self-sustains indefinitely, and is particularly charming growing from walls and between paving stones where organic soil would be unsuitable. Related to the blue C. flexuosa and yellow C. lutea, it occupies the same garden role as the yellow species but in white — longer-blooming and more shade-tolerant than many competitors.

planting.note: Plant small corms just below the soil surface (1 inch deep) in humus-rich, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil under deciduous trees; spring cyclamen grows and flowers in winter and spring while deciduous trees are leafless, then goes dormant in summer when tree shade intensifies. Dry summer dormancy is essential — do not water during this period.

directions: Plant corms just below the surface in humus-rich, well-drained soil in full shade under deciduous trees; spring cyclamen is the third species of hardy garden cyclamen (after the autumn-blooming C. hederifolium and the winter-blooming C. coum) — a Mediterranean native of olive groves and deciduous woodlands in southern Europe that flowers in spring (March–April) with elegantly reflexed, rose-pink to carmine, fragrant flowers above beautifully silver-marbled, ivy-shaped leaves. Like its relatives, it grows actively in winter and spring when deciduous tree canopies are open, then retreats into summer dormancy under the dense shade of summer foliage. The flowers have a subtle, sweet fragrance uncommon in this genus. Hardier than the florist cyclamen (C. persicum) but somewhat less cold-tolerant than C. hederifolium, thriving in sheltered woodland gardens in zones 7–9. Together, C. hederifolium, C. coum, and C. repandum extend hardy cyclamen interest from August through April — nearly year-round in mild gardens.

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring before flowering or plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; Young's barrenwort is semi-evergreen to deciduous — cut back old foliage to the ground in late winter before new growth and flowers emerge. It tolerates dry shade under trees better than most shade perennials once established.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part to full shade; Young's barrenwort is a hybrid epimedium (between E. diphyllum and E. grandiflorum) producing some of the most delicate and refined foliage and flowers in the genus. The heart-shaped, finely textured leaves emerge in spring with attractive bronze-red coloring before maturing to fresh, light green, then turning bronzy-crimson in autumn. In spring the flowers rise just above the new leaves on wiry stems — small, spurred, in 'bishop's hat' form in pure white ('Niveum') or soft pink ('Roseum') — giving the plant a quality of refined, airy delicacy unlike the bolder epimedium species. It spreads steadily by rhizomes to form a carpet in shaded areas and tolerates the difficult dry-shade conditions under large trees where few groundcovers thrive. Part of the invaluable epimedium genus — collectively the finest group of shade groundcovers for dry, difficult woodland conditions with year-round seasonal interest.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring or summer, or start from seed indoors; dittany of Crete requires sharply drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun. It is sensitive to overwatering and wet winter conditions. In zones 8–10 it is perennial; in colder climates, grow as a container plant brought indoors before frost or overwinter cuttings.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; dittany of Crete is one of the most distinctive and ornamental of all Mediterranean herbs — a native of the rocky hillsides of Crete and the eastern Aegean producing small, rounded, densely woolly, silver-gray leaves so thickly covered with white hairs that the plant appears to be made of pale velvet or silver felt, with a warm, spicy aromatic scent when brushed. In summer it produces nodding clusters of small pink to purple flowers surrounded by overlapping, hop-like, purple-green bracts in elegant dangling clusters — both the foliage and flowers being remarkably ornamental. It has been valued since antiquity: Aristotle described wild goats eating it to remove arrows from wounds; it appears in Virgil and Pliny as a wound herb; and it is still used in Cretan herbal tea and liqueur (Cretan digestif). Outstanding as a trailing container plant where the woolly, nodding flower clusters can cascade over the pot edge. Related to culinary oregano but much more ornamental than culinary.
